content.full_text:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the ..Act,).
QUESTION
Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from participatin gin a
governmental decision involving at application for a conditional use permit to extend the
disposable area boundaries of a landfill where the new boundaries will be located within
3,000 feet of your property?
Farn PolrrrcAt- PRACTTcES CounnssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 e Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (9t6) 322-0886
October 5,2006
Robert L. Roos
2550 Homestead Road
Templeton, CA93465
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4-06-177
Dear Mr. Roos:
CONCLUSION
No. The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions do not prohibit you from
participatingin a governmental decision involving an application for a conditional use
permit to extend the disposal area boundaries of a landfill because the real property in
which you have an economic interest is located more than 500 feet from the boundaries
of the property that is the subject of the governmental decision.
FACTS
You are an appointed member of the San Luis Obispo Planning Commission (the
"Planning Commission"). The Planning Commission is considering an application by a
local landfill operator for a conditional user permit to extend the life of anLxisting
landfill by expanding the allowable disposal area footprint from the existing 38.44 acres
to 82.74 acres (the "Project'). The practical effect of this permit, if approvid, would be
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations aryearat Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
.'@-"n


File No. A-06-177
Page No. 2
to extend the life of the existing landfill from the year 20l6,when it is estimated to be
full, to the year 2045. No changes are proposed to the methods of operation - such as
hours of operation, maximum daily vehicles allowed, or daily tonnaie allowed.
Your principal residence is located on the same road on which the landfill is
located. The entrance to the landfill is about 2,loofeet from your driveway. you have
lived in your home since 7977; the landfill has been there since the early 1670,s. The
edge of your property is above 2,000 feet from the existing landfill footprint, 1,500 from
qroperty currently owned by the applicant and used as a buffer ur"u, uri about3,000 feet
from the closest portion of the proposed expansion area. Neither the existinglandfill nor
the proposed expansion area is visible from your property. you are affectedly the
landfill operation be-cause vehicles going to and from the landfill use the road on which
you live, and noise frory tle landfill operations - earthmoving machines and backup-
beepers, can occasionally be heard at your residence.
An environmeltal impact report ("EIR") has been prepared that describes the
Project, its effect on the environment and lists mitigation rir"urrrr", and alternatives to the
Project' The EIR has found no class 1 effects and f,as proposed several mitigation
measures to decrease the effects to less than signifi"*i l"r"lr. The logic ofio
unmitigatable effects_seems to hinge on the faitthatno change to the methods of
operation of the landfill are proposed, and only the allowable disposal area footprint
would be increased - allowing the landfill to siay in operation foi many -or" y"ur..
ANALYSIS
PornNrmr, CoNrr,tcr or INTEREsT
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will ..perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused uy ttreir owr, finanlial interests
or the financial interests ofpersons wlo have supported them.,, (Section g1001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits anypublic official from-making,participatrng in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influenc"f gor"*ental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disquali$zing conflict of inierest. (Regulation rszoolu;.; rhe generat
rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenJver a public offi"iut makes a
goveflrmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.
srpps I & 2z Anr You A Purr.rc orrrcrar, M,rrcNc, pnnrrcrpanrNc rN M,rroN6, on
INrr,urNcING A GovrnNnrrNTAL DrcrsroN?
As an appointed member of thes_an Luis obispo Planning commission, you are a
public official under the Act. (Section s204g.) consequently, yJu may not -uk",


File No. A-06-177
Page No. 3
pattiapate in making, or otherwise use your official position to influenc e anydecisions that
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your economic
interests. As a mernber of the Planning Commission, you will bscall"d rpo, to consider
the application for a conditional use permit to expand ihe allowable disposal area of the
landfill. Therefore, you will be making, participiting !n making, or otherwise using your
official position to influence a goveflrmental deiision.2
Srnp 3: Do You I{ava A PoTENTTALLv DrseuALrFyrNG EcoNoMrc INrnnrsr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amaterial
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, on
a member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, "*pl,oy"", or holds anyposition of
management (section 87 I 03 (d); regulation lg7 03 .l (b));
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 1g703.2);
o An economic interest in any source of income, includingpromised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section g7103(c);
regulation 18703.3);
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $360
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(e); r"giLtii"
18703.9;
a
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects,, rule
(section 87 103 ; regulation 1 3703.5).
2 If a public official's office is listed insection 87200 (*87200 filers,, include planning
commission members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public Leeting, then
he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identisz each type of economic
interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation
18702'5(bxl)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave thI room for the
duration ofthe discussion andlor vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found io r"g,riutlo, lg7I2.s,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)


File No. A-06-177
Page No. 4
You indicate that you have an interest in the real property upon which your
principal residence is located. For purposes of this anatysis, w" urr^r . you have a direct
interest in the real property worth $2,000 or more. Accordingly, you have an economic
interest in this real property under section 87103 and regulat ion 1g703.2.
You have not presented any facts that would indicate any other economic interest.
Accordingly, our analysis is limited to your economic interest in yow residence.3
STEp 4: Ann Youn EcoNourc INrrnpsrs Drnpcrr,y m.INDTRECTLy rxvolvro rN
rnr GovTRNMENTAL DrcrsroN?
"fn order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
frnancial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the
goverrmental deci sion. " (Regul at ion I 87 04 (a).)
Real Property: Regulation l8704.2lists the factors that xeused to determine
whether an economic interest in real property is directly or indirectly involved in a
governmental decision.
Regulation 18704.2 then provides:
"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the
following apply:
(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision. For purposes of subdivision (aX5), real property is
located '\Mithin 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundarie.j of in"
real property which is the subject of the goremmental decision,, if any part
of the real property is within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment prdect area.
(2)The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inilusion in oi
exclusion from any city, county, diskict or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affectingthe real property. For purposes of this
subdivision, the terms "zoriflg" and'tezoning" sharl refer to the act of
establishing or changing the zoningor land use designation on the real
property in which the official has an interest.
3 A public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances is not considered when the
effect is on real property. (Regulation 18705.5.)


File No. A-06-177
Page No. 5
(3) The goveflrmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authoizing a
specific use or uses of the real property in which the official has an
interest.
(a) The govemmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real iroperty
in which the official has an interest.
(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopmenf area, or to
rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which
the official has an interest, or any part of it is located within the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area.
(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
seryices."
Because your property is located more than 500 feet from the boundaries of the
property that is the subject of the governmental decision, and the decision does not
involve any of the other factors listed in regulation 18704.2 (a)Q-6) with respect to the
real property in which you have an interest, your property is indirectly involved in the
governmental deci sion.
srrps 5 aNo 6: M,rrrnr,q.Lrrv sraNoARD AND Fmrsnnasrlrry
Real Property: Regulation 18705.2(b) sets forth the materiality standard pertinent
to real property that is indirectly involved in the decision.
Indirectly Iqvolved Real Propertlr: For indirectly involved real property interests,
other than leaseholds, the financial effect of a governmental decision on ttre reat property
is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are
specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the
nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic interest, which
make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
the real property in which the public official has an economic interest.a However, we
a An effect upon economic interests is considered'?easonably foreseeable" if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether the financial consequences of a
goverunental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made depends on the
facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably
foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (lg7r 1 FppC Ops. iOS.y


File No. A-06-177
Page No. 6
have advised that in order to rebut this presumption of nonm ateiality,the effect of the
governmental decision must result in a clearly distinguishable and substantial effect on
the official's real property. (Berger Advice Letter,No. A-05-05a.)
Based upon the facts presented, it does not appear that this presumption has been
rebutted. If this is the case, you do not have a conflict of interest under the Act in
participating in the decision described herein.s
sTrps 7 a,No 8: Pusr,rc GrNrrur,r,y & LEGALLy REeuTRED pARTrcrpATroN
Because it does not appear that you have a conflict of interest, neither the ..public
generally" or "legally required participation" exceptions would be applicable here.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
John W. Wallace
General Counsel
\
By:
Lenkeit
Senior
Counsel
Legal Division
WJLjg
I:\Adviceltrs\06-177
5 The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (lg7i) I
FPPC Ops. 71; section 83114.)

CONCLUSION: No. The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions do not prohibit you from

permit to extend the disposal area boundaries of a landfill because the real property in
which you have an economic interest is located more than 500 feet from the boundaries
